data.title: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets in the developing world, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, slipped 0.2% in the first quarter. The months' long rally across equity markets in the developing world—fueled in part by global investors seeking alternatives to US risk assets—came to an abrupt end in March due to escalating tensions in the Middle East, where the US's and Israel's joint military campaign against Iran curbed risk appetites. News that Iran had imposed a near-total blockade of the Strait of Hormuz in response to the airstrikes concerned many investors, who feared that its effective closure might have dire consequences for the global economy given that one-fifth of the world's oil and natural gas and roughly one-third of the world's fertilizer that sustains global crops pass through the narrow waterway. Global stock and oil markets were volatile throughout March, as investors searched for any signs that a de-escalation of hostilities was on the horizon. The price of Brent crude oil, the global benchmark, ended March at US$118.45 per barrel, a 63% increase since the war in Iran began on 28 February, and the largest monthly jump in percentage terms on record.

Emerging Asia, which is heavily reliant on crude oil from the Middle East, was the worst-performing region in the period, falling 1.5%, due largely to worries that the oil shock will adversely impact the region's economic outlook. Nevertheless, stock markets in global technology hubs Korea and Taiwan both rose sharply, thanks to the demand upcycle for memory chips. China's stock market fell as a result of a sell-off of AI-linked stocks. India's stock market fell on concerns about the impact that surging oil prices will have on the country's economy. Indonesia's stock market tumbled on news that index provider MSCI was considering removing the country as a constituent of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index due to low liquidity and ownership concentrations, which, if carried out, would require exchange-traded funds (ETFs) to remove Indonesian company shares to match the index's composition and weighting.

Latin America was the best-performing region, rising 14.6%, thanks largely to the region's limited exposure to crude oil from the Middle East. Colombia's stock market climbed, thanks to its status as a net oil exporter. Brazil's stock market rose sharply on bullish sentiment about the country's economic outlook and expectations that the country's central bank would soon start a rate-cutting cycle. Peru's stock market gained in sympathy with rising commodity prices.

The region encompassing emerging Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) also outperformed, despite moving sideways. South Africa's stock market fell in sympathy with the steep drop in gold prices in March and on concerns about the country's economic outlook stemming from the country's heavy dependence on crude oil from the Middle East. Saudi Arabia's stock market rose in sympathy with soaring crude oil prices.

Communication services, consumer discretionary, and real estate were the worst-performing sectors. Information technology, energy, and utilities were the best-performing sectors.

(Index returns were measured net of taxes and in US dollar terms.)

Portfolio Review

In the first quarter, the Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio rose in absolute terms and outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. (Excess return was measured net of fees and in US dollar terms.)

Helped

  • Stock selection in the energy and financials sectors boosted relative performance, as did stock selection in Korea and China.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the energy sector and to Brazil and Mexico contributed.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposure to the communication services and consumer discretionary sectors and to Korea and India were beneficial to relative performance.
  • Shares of ASE Technology (ASX) (1.4% Portfolio weighting) traded higher after the Taiwan-based chip testing and packaging provider reported better-than-expected full-year 2025 results, highlighted by earnings that grew 25% from a year earlier. Shares were boosted further after the company issued an upbeat outlook as it gains market share in advanced packaging services.
  • Brazil-based Oil producers Petroleo Brasileiro (PBR) (Petrobras) (1.6% Portfolio weighting) and Petro Rio (1.5% Portfolio weighting) saw their stock prices appreciate after reporting strong operational performance in the fourth quarter. Shares received an additional boost in sympathy with the surge in the price of oil.
  • Shares of Weichai Power (WEICF) (0.7% Portfolio weighting) gained on bullish sentiment about the China-based diesel engine manufacturer's upside potential based on opportunities to provide power generation for artificial intelligence AI data centers.
  • Shares of SK Hynix (3.0% Portfolio weighting) rose after the Korea-based semiconductor manufacturer reported better-than-expected quarterly earnings, underscoring ongoing strength in the price of memory chips.

Hurt

  • Stock selection in the information technology sector undercut relative performance, as did stock selection in Taiwan.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the consumer staples and financials sectors and to South Africa detracted.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposure to the information technology sector and to Saudi Arabia and China and India were detrimental to relative performance.
  • Shares of Wivynn Corp (1.6% Portfolio weighting) traded lower after the Taiwan-based cloud information technology (IT) infrastructure provider reported weaker-than-expected fourth-quarter gross margins. Shares were weighed down further after the company announced a larger-than-expected issuance of convertible bonds.
  • Shares of Infosys (INFY) (1.1% Portfolio weighting) and Tata Consultancy (1.2% Portfolio weighting) fell as part of a broader market sell-off of IT service provider stocks on concerns about the capabilities of Anthropic's Claude code, which writes and tests code quickly with natural language prompts.
  • Shares of UPL (0.7% Portfolio weighting) fell on profit-taking after a period of strong stock-price appreciation stemming from news that the India-based agricultural chemicals manufacturer was exploring an initial public offering of its seeds business.
  • Shares of Axis Bank (AXBKY) (0.9% Portfolio weighting) fell on profit-taking after a period of strong stock-price appreciation in the wake of the India-based lender reporting better-than-expected quarterly earnings supported by robust loan growth.
  • Shares of insurer Sanlam (SLMAF) (1.0% Portfolio weighting) fell as part of a broader sell-off of South Africa's stock market due to concerns that the surging price of crude oil will adversely impact the country's economic outlook. South Africa is heavily reliant on oil imported from the Middle East.

Outlook

Market fundamentals remain fluid based upon significant new and existing geopolitical and trade risks. Our expectations will vary based upon these issues and valuations, but, at this juncture, we remain reasonably constructive on long-term prospects for emerging markets, especially if global growth remains positive and balanced.

data.title: Investing For Life: Why Your Portfolio Is Your Most Valuable Asset Ever
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Cash flow is valuable. That's something that every investor understands, and as companies are reporting their earnings over the next few weeks, we will be pulling up the 10-Qs and turning to the statement of cash flows. Sure, the "EPS" and other headline numbers are important as well, but the cash flows are where we find out how strong a company truly is. High earnings are great, but earnings that are being converted into free cash flow are the most valuable.

Most investors understand this, and you'll often see analysts in earnings calls asking questions about cash flows and what the cash will be used for. A company with significant excess cash flow that can be redirected as needed is a substantially better investment than a company that has minimal cash flow or cash flows that are already claimed for essential payments like interest.

So, why do we talk about our portfolio differently? Your stock portfolio is the most important business in the market for you. Sure, "book value", the theoretical liquidation value that your portfolio is trading at, is an interesting factoid. You can borrow against it, which I wouldn't recommend, or you can sell a piece of it to get cash. Yet, odds are you aren't going to sell every stock in your portfolio today. The current liquidation value, for practical purposes, is a number you don't intend to realize. It's a number that changes every minute that the market is open, and you have no way of knowing what it will be in 5 minutes, let alone 5 years.

I treat my portfolio like every other business. It generates revenues, it has expenses, and it pays me a dividend. My portfolio is a company that is in the business of generating investment cash flow for me.

Today, I want to take a look at the primary reasons I invest for cash flow.

Reason #1: Get Paid Today

Perhaps one of the most overlooked values of dividends is the time value of money. Money today is more valuable than money tomorrow. We understand this intuitively. Say I offered to pay you $1,000 a year from now or $500 now and the rest in a year, which would you prefer?

An investment that provides cash flow throughout the investment is more valuable than one that doesn't provide cash flow. The reason is that the cash flow can be used. One way of calculating this is to look at the IRR, or "Internal Rate of Return."

There are many calculators available on the Internet. Here is a look at an example. Which is better:

  1. You invest $100,000 in a dividend-paying investment that pays 10% per year for four years and then cuts its dividend to 8% of the original capital before being sold for $80,000 in year 8. Your total realized return is $152,000 on a $100,000 investment.
  2. You invest $100,000 in an investment that provides zero cash flows and sell it in year 8 for $174,500.

The answer is that the two investments have essentially the same internal rate of return. Here is the dividend investment: Source

Here is the investment with no cash flow:

Despite the latter having a higher gross total return, the dividend investment has a similar internal return because money is being extracted each year. And money has value. It is fungible, and it can be used for a number of different things, including making new investments.

Reason #2: Realized Gains Decrease Risk

Dividends are small realized gains that you realize with each payment. It is tangible cash that is coming out of the company and going into your hand. You can then decide whether to put that money at risk or not.

Consider one of my holdings that is going through a big downswing right now. Here is Eagle Point Credit Company Inc (ECC) since January 2021, with no dividends reinvested:

Horrible, right? It's down 63%; that's a lot. On the cash flow side, ECC has produced over $10,000 in dividends over this period. More than 100% of the initial investment.

Materially more than the index produced. So when comparing investments, you are looking at one that returned over $10,000 in cash to you and one that hasn't. What if we compared them with an apples-to-apples comparison where we withdrew the same amount of money? Let's withdraw $1,800/year, adjusting for inflation.

Suddenly, the difference isn't that large. In fact, until January 2026, ECC was outperforming materially.

When you match cash flows so that the same amount of money is being withdrawn, suddenly the indices lose their luster. When looking at your returns, it is important to be aware of the difference in cash flows between investments and ensure that you are comparing apples to apples—investments that have similar cash flows.

If you bought ECC in January 2021 and withdrew the dividends, it is literally impossible for you to have a negative total return regardless of what happens. If you bought an S&P 500 ETF, it is very possible to have a negative total return if the stock market declines over 40%. Which is a lot, but it is hardly an unprecedented amount.

The great thing about dividend-paying investments is that you get the opportunity to reaffirm your acceptance of the risk with each dividend. You can reinvest in the same company or a different company or keep the cash. If at any point you think conditions are unfavorable or the price is too high, you can just stop reinvesting and collect dividends—each one reducing your exposure to the risks inherent in the investment.

Reason #3: Cash is Flexible

We alluded to it above: cash is fungible. You get cash in your account, and you can use it for any number of things. The Wall Street complex is insistent that you need to reinvest everything forever. This makes sense because most on Wall Street make money from AUM (assets under management). They want you to invest your money, leave it invested forever, pass it on to your kids, who will hopefully also leave it invested forever, and take out a minimal amount.

Well, if you don't intend on ever using the money, why do you care about your investment returns at all? Why are you wasting your time?

Most of us are investing because we have some current or future financial need or desire. The most common reason is to retire in comfort. Yet, so many retire and sit there with their investments tied up in stocks that produce nothing. They fear selling because they know the stock market can crash. They fear running out of money. So the nest egg sits there, untouched, or only a minimal amount is withdrawn because investors don't know how much is safe to withdraw.

It's your money; you worked hard to build it up—it's ok to spend some of it.

Dividends coming into your portfolio provide you with a defined revenue. You know how much is coming in, and it's recurring. You can then use that cash for whatever your needs are. You want to reinvest, great. Do you want to reinvest in the same investments? Add new investments? Take a greater risk? Make your portfolio more conservative? Take some cash out for personal spending?

The choice is yours.

Conclusion: Managing Your Cash Flow Like a Business

Interest and dividends are the revenues of your portfolio's business. Like any other business, they aren't set in stone. Some will be explicitly variable, such as Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), a CEF (closed-end fund) that adjusts its dividend quarterly based on changes in NAV (net asset value).

Some will provide lower dividends up front but will provide consistent growth. Realty Income (O), "The Monthly Dividend Company", is a REIT in our portfolio that is part of the Dividend Aristocrats.

Other investments will pay 100% of their cash flow and will see their dividends adjust relatively frequently. Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) is a mortgage REIT that has paid a quarterly dividend that has ranged from a peak of $3.00 to a low of $0.40.

Still others will pay a relatively flat amount and only change their dividend occasionally, like PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY).

Your income will not always be consistent, but like a business, if you withdraw a portion of your cash flow and reinvest the rest, your portfolio will produce more income next year.

The result looks something like this. Here are these four holdings, from 2003 to 2026. Withdrawing $7,500 in year one and increasing withdrawals to match inflation. All excess dividends are reinvested:

Because the payout was conservative, the portfolio always produced more dividends than were withdrawn. Over time, the income grew, and it grew faster than inflation.

In fact, by 2025, the scheduled withdrawal of $13,435 was less than half of the dividends the portfolio produced:

This means that the investor can comfortably give themselves a raise.

Past results aren't a guarantee of the future. Dividends do get cut; they got cut in this sample portfolio. We recommend withdrawing a maximum of 75% of the cash flow your portfolio is producing to allow for reinvestment and to provide a cushion for when things go wrong.

However, you will know the day you withdraw cash that your portfolio produced it. You will know how close you are to the guidelines, and you can take action if something is entering into a danger zone. If you need to make changes, you can make them because cash flow gives you an objective measure to determine how your portfolio is performing.

Manage your portfolio like a business, and it will reward you like a business. That's The Income Method.

data.title: SLM : Cheap Valuation Meets Rising Credit Risk
data.description:
Introduction

In my view, SLM corporation is a cheaply valued, quality profit-generating stock, though the current upside depends on whether the worsening loan quality will remain managed and won‘t make this value a trap.

SLM Corporation (SLM) takes up a dominating part of the US private student loans. The company’s 2026 Q1 results beat the market’s expectations: reached $1.54 EPS compared to $1.40 an year ago. This growth encouraged the management to raise guidance for the whole of 2026 to the $3.10-3.20 range.

The main profitability engine remains the net interest margin, which reached 5.29%, and the cost of funds decreased to 4.13%. Such a dynamic shows an efficient balance management in high interest rate environment, allowing to maintain a profitable difference between borrowing and lending prices. Despite the strong financials, the market valuations remain a conservative P/E ratio of 6.7x, which is below the ten year average.

This suggests that investors have doubts about the worsening credit quality: the level of overdue loans increased to 3.98%, compared to 3.58% year earlier. The company reacted by increasing the level of provisions to 6.05% of the total portfolio, trying to ensure itself against possible losses.

The company‘s priority still remains its capital return strategy, where in 2026 Q1 shares worth $259 million were bought back and a dividend of $0.13 was paid out. Even though issuance of new loans grew 5% and reached $2.9 billion, the increasing operating costs reflect the balance between the strong cash flow generation and the risk tied to the solvency of younger borrowers.

Business overview

SLM Corporation's business model is focused on supplying private student loans. The company‘s main segment is generating revenues through interest margin, which is reaching 5.29%, and in the company‘s product portfolio, the dominating loans are for bachelor's and master's studies, which make up more than 90% of the company's assets.

Company strategic rotation is based on an originate-and-sell model, whose goal is to optimize the capital structure and manage the balance sheet volume. In 2026, the management confirmed a plan to sell loan portfolios for an additional $1 billion, seeking to realize the profit from the sale and reduce risk-weighted assets.

Even though the company is trying to diversify its revenues through credit cards, such as Sallie Mae Ignite or Evolve, as well as high-yield savings accounts, these products remain auxiliary and serve more as client acquisition tools rather than main profit engines.

The average return on private education loans is reaching 10.46%, taking away the 4.13% financing costs, which creates a stable cash flow, though also increases the concentration risk of being in one sector. Such a structure means that SLM results are directly dependent on the US higher education price dynamics and changes in federal student loan policies.

The efficiency indicator in 2026 Q1 was at 30.6%, which shows that operational costs are growing more slowly than revenues from the main segment. Its rotation between the loan issuance and its transfer on the secondary market allows the company to maintain a high ROE, which is over 56%, though it leaves it sensitive to the secondary market‘s liquidity fluctuations.

Valuation

SLM Corporation shares are traded at 6.6x TTM P/E and a forward P/E of around 7.5x. This valuation is a lot lower than the 10 year historical average, which shows that the market still values the sustainability of student loan sector growth with caution, despite the fact that the company beat 2026 Q1 earnings forecasts.

The company‘s P/B ratio is reaching 2.1x, which is well above a direct comptitor Navient (NAVI), whose valuation remains low due to the negative net income and decreasing portfolio. Such SLM‘s premium against Navient is justified by a very high return on common equity, which in 2026 Q1 reached 56.4%, and having a dominating part of the market in the private student loan segment.

Compared to Sofi Technologies (SOFI), whose P/E ratio is over 47X, SLM remains valued as a traditional financial institution, rather than a technology platform, its shares are valued more as a value rather than a growth category asset.

The company‘s aggressive capital return policy, including 12 million share buyback in Q1 and a $200 million accelerated buyback program, directly maintains EPS growth, though 5.29% net income margin shows that the operational profitability remains sensitive to interest rate fluctuations.

he current valuation suggests that the market has already priced in the planned 12-14% loan issuance growth therefore, in my view, a further multiple expansion is unlikely without essential federal reforms in the field of study financing.

Risks

SLM Corporation is showing clear signs of worsening credit quality, which is currently masked in reports as one-time accounting actions. Even though the company reported earnings of $1.54 per share, the level of late payments in 2026 Q1 grew to 3.98%, compared to 3.58% last year. Also, there was an increased share of hardship-enduring borrowers, reaching 0.99%.

These numbers show that the base portfolio is experiencing pressure due to the impact of high interest rates on graduates' solvency. Especially risky is that in 2026 Q1 profit was artificially supported by $11 million in negative provisions, which happened not from the improved borrowers behavior, but from the $3.33 billion loan sale and tied to that $131 million release of reserves.

Without these one time deals, the net income margin, which is reaching 5.29%, should shrink even further, because the company has accumulated significant liquidity, whose yield is lower. The current P/E ratio of around 6.6 reflects the market‘s understanding that profit growth is dependent on an aggressive sale of assets, rather than sustainable interest revenue expansion.

If the level of write-offs, which was at $89 million in Q1, is over the forecasted $345-385 annual range, the company‘s ability to maintain the share buyback program will be limited, which would eliminate the main stock price support factor.

Thesis summary

In my view, SLM Corporation is currently not expensive, highly profitable, but clearly has certain risks. The market values the company at around 6.6x P/E even though the business is still generating a high ROE, stable EPS growth, and is actively buying back shares. Such indicators usually signal about a unrecognized opportunity or that investors are seeing a problem in the future. In my opinion, with this stock, both options are true at the same time.

The positive side is clear. The company is dominating private student loans in the market, has a strong scale effect, good margin, and a disciplined capital distribution. While the US labor market remains stable, and loan demand is not dropping, the company can generate solid profits as well as return capital to its shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

The weaker side of things is also clear. The worsening loan quality, increasing delays, and pressure on the solvency of younger borrowers show that the current profitability could be at its highest point. If the economy slows down or unemployment among young professionals grows, credit losses could rise rapidly.

Therefore, in my opinion, this is not a risk-free, cheap stock. This is more of a value idea with a cyclical risk. If credit quality stabilizes, the upside could be very strong. If it worsens further, the market may be correct about assigning this low multiple to the company.

data.title: I Won't Buy POET Technologies' Dip (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
POET Technologies Stock - Downgrading My Bullish Rating

In mid-January 2026, I published an article on POET Technologies Inc. (POET), calling the stock a "Buy" because the prospects and the technology it had developed by that time seemed quite solid, promising future contract wins with major AI enablers and, as a result, an inflection in POET's financial performance over the coming few years. The stock price didn't move for some time, but then suddenly, it soared by >100% as the prospects became clearer to more people. But yesterday, all of a sudden, the stock fell down by >47%, and it keeps contracting on the post-market:

The reason for the decline is so significant - it's not just an ordinary earnings miss or something of that nature - that I have decided to downgrade my bullish rating to "Sell" and never look back at POET again.

POET Stock - My Reasoning For A Sell

Usually, when a promising stock loses so much in a single trading session, I look to increase my exposure on the dip. However, in the case of POET, the underlying reason for its stock price meltdown indicates that I should not do so. According to Seeking Alpha, POET "disclosed it had lost a contract with Celestial AI, which has been acquired by Marvell Technology (MRVL)."

"As the basis for the cancellation, Marvell indicated that the Company had made disclosures of information related to the Purchase Order and shipping information in contravention of its confidentiality obligations," POET said in a statement.

POET added that it remains focused on "executing its strategic priorities and advancing product development within the AI and optical networking markets to meet increasing demand."

Previously, I had a thesis that POET's fab-lite manufacturing model could be attractive to larger tech players. And MRVL was basically giving my thesis a sort of validation when they acquired Celestial AI at a premium (for ~$3.25 billion) - Celestial AI was a well-known partner of POET. I thought that if POET could successfully scale its 800G and 1.6T optical engines, it would be ready to capitalize on the massive TAM, with hyperscalers willing to engage at scale.

Everything broke when POET's CFO, Thomas Mika, appeared on a Stocktwits YouTube interview on April 21 and decided to defend the company's legitimacy against the short report that came out from a well-known short seller. It is not important for the story to repeat the filing of that report, in my opinion. What matters is the livestream conversation - Mika explicitly named Marvell Technology, stating that POET had secured an active purchase order to supply high-bandwidth light sources for Celestial AI's photonic fabric. The problem is that executives cannot publicly disclose such information if an NDA has been signed, and there was indeed an NDA between MRVL and POET, so the former sent written notice terminating all Celestial AI purchase orders.

Marvell stated the cancellation was because POET had wrongly shared confidential details, so it's not about POET's tech itself, which is at least some good news for POET shareholders. But the level of execution is what makes me stay aside.

POET is a pre-revenue growth company that had a chance to stick with much larger Marvell, then possibly scaling further and serving similarly-sized (or bigger) entities. And now, this opportunity has vanished, in my understanding. Tier-1 companies partner with a crystal clear reputation, so the very fact that POET's CFO - one of the key personnel - violated the simplest discipline rule is already a major red flag for future contract signings.

Without POET, Marvell can rely on its recently acquired Polariton, which is now developing in-house optical interconnect solutions for them - they don't need a middleman for their stack now, so the deal termination is unlikely to affect them that much. They can shift their packaging and laser sourcing directly to other suppliers, likely to Sivers Semiconductors (SIVEF) or similar players. But without the anchor customer (Celestial AI), POET is basically left fundamentally broken (at least I view it like that).

Again, the loss of Celestial AI will leave POET with a few million dollars in orders, which is really too small to validate mass production. Assuming Tier-1 giants like NVIDIA (NVDA) or Broadcom (AVGO), who could potentially replace POET, will now be incredibly wary of entering into co-development agreements with a management team that leaks confidential shipping details, I think POET can survive only if it fires the whole management team. If the Board proceeds with that decision, it's going to be the other side of the sword - the market doesn't like uncertainties that come from the immediate management change. In chess, players call situations like this "zugzwang", which can be translated from German as "forced movement." POET is exactly at that point right now, and I'd not be willing to be among its shareholders that will likely suffer more from the seemingly inevitable rise of uncertainty ahead.

Final Words

If you think that POET has already absorbed all the negatives, I'm here to tell you that the market's consensus data still shows quite positive projections for the firm's FY2027 sales, which I think are unlikely to materialize now.

There's no need to talk about POET's valuation because it's a pre-revenue company that has just lost its key client. The stock price is more pictures - following yesterday's meltdown, POET is still 800%+ above its 2023 lows:

The pressure is here to stay, in my opinion, so POET can carry on yesterday's price action for longer than dip-buyers might be expecting right now. Based on that, I'm downgrading POET to "Sell."

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Medical Properties Trust: From Crisis To Comeback
data.description:
Even though I have significantly reduced my exposure to the company in recent months in favor of some more attractive prospects, one firm that I still feel very bullish about right now is Medical Properties Trust (MPT). In my last article about the firm, published in December of last year, I made the decision to reaffirm it as a ‘strong buy’ candidate. Tenant issues had been largely resolved, and we were seeing a return to growth. This is not to say that everything was perfect about the institution. The amount of net debt that it had in relation to other similar firms was still quite high. But yet, the business was trading on the cheap in relation to cash flows and its book value. Fast forward to today, and we have seen some additional data come in. And even though some aspects of the data ended up being mixed, the general trend is positive. On the whole, I believe that the enterprise certainly deserves the ‘strong buy’ rating I assigned it, even if its risk profile is not exactly the lowest out there.

Even though I remain bullish about the entity, that bullishness could change based on new data that comes through. The good news is that, before the market opens on April 30th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, analysts actually seem quite bullish that management's strategy is playing out as intended. However, anybody who has followed the company closely knows that the firm has thrown investors for a loop from time to time. So I would encourage everyone to keep a close eye on the data that does become available.

Medical Properties Trust is on the mend

If you follow Medical Properties Trust closely, odds are that you are well aware of the business model of the business and the troubles it has faced in recent years. For the uninitiated, the enterprise operates as a REIT that acquires and develops net leased healthcare facilities. At the end of 2025, the business had investments in 384 facilities, with those accounting for roughly 39,000 licensed beds spread across 31 states, as well as throughout seven countries in Europe, and in Colombia. Even though this might seem like an industry that would be incredibly stable, the last few years have seen challenges for the company. This has included bankruptcies associated with major tenants that have struggled with excessive leverage and weak medical revenue in a post COVID world. Some likely view that the end result for Medical Properties Trust would be bankruptcy also considering the exposure that it had to deal with and the large amount of net debt on its books. However, management seems to have pulled through these difficult times.

The newest data provided by management is certainly encouraging. For the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue for the firm came in at $270.3 million. That was up nicely from the $231.8 million reported a year earlier. This was in spite of the fact that straight line rent income actually contracted from $43.7 million to $36 million. In fact, the kind of growth that we saw from the institution was the best kind. Rent billed grew nicely from $167 million to $212.5 million.

This requires some context. After some of its tenants went into bankruptcy, the company ended up re-tenanting some of the properties that those tenants had controlled. For instance, during the final quarter of 2025, the company entered into a new lease for six California hospitals that were previously operated by Prospect Medical Holdings. This particular example is more of a forward-looking item considering that future rent is the focus of this arrangement given the timing that we are dealing with. But management did mention that, by December of this year, annual rent coming from these properties will be about $45 million.

The firm also completed a restructuring transaction with Vibra Healthcare that is resulting in a new 20 year master lease and brought in $18 million in the form of a one time rent payment. We also have other changes that the company has made to its portfolio. During the quarter, as an example, the company acquired a single post-acute facility here in the US in exchange for $32 million. And it bought up another post-acute facility in Europe in February of this year for €23 million that will also boost rent moving forward. The goal from these transactions is for the company to grow its annualized cash rent to at least $1 billion by the end of this year. And as we can see from the quarterly results, the business is certainly on the way to achieving that.

As revenue for the company improves, profitability is rising also. Because this is a REIT, I don't really put much value on net profits. But for context, the firm did go from a net loss of $412.8 million to a net profit of $17.3 million. That loss had been caused by a $387 million real estate and other asset impairment charge, and by a $48.7 million fair value adjustment on securities that negatively impacted it. But when we focus on cash flows instead, we did see some improvements. Operating cash flow grew from $76.7 million to $160.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a jump from $45 million to $88.2 million. FFO, or funds from operations, improved from negative $36.1 million to positive $115.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a small decline from $107.7 million to $106.6 million. The good news is that, even with that being the case, EBITDA for the business improved from $216.4 million to $254.9 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 compared to 2024. Although cash flows were mostly lower year over year, at least the final quarter that I covered already shows that the picture has started to improve at last. This means to me that we are just coming out of the very bottom of the trough that the company was in. And for those looking to buy in on the cheap, that is the optimal time to jump in.

Even though the overall picture for the business is improving here and the aforementioned changes that management made should boost revenue and profitability moving forward, this is not to say that the company is out of the woods entirely. In the chart above, you can see the change in net debt for the company and its net leverage over the last several quarters. The good news here is that the net leverage ratio for the company ended up falling from 10.16 in the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year to 9 in the final quarter. That actually brings the company down to the lowest point that it has been since the second quarter of 2024. But as the chart below illustrates, the net leverage ratio for the company is still quite a bit higher than what we see when looking at other similar enterprises. This means additional risk that needs to be reflected in the valuation of the company. And sure enough, that is precisely what we observe when valuing the company accordingly.

In the chart below, you can see how the company is currently valued on an EV to EBITDA basis. The 11.9 reading that we get here is actually quite attractive for a REIT. The other players that I am comparing it to are trading at multiples of between 17.1 and 31.6. I personally would feel comfortable saying that the stock is fairly valued, considering its risk profile, at an EV to EBITDA multiple of around 15. That would mean upside from where the stock is trading at right now of about 106.8%.

Perhaps a more appropriate way to value the company would be through the price to book multiple. In the chart below, you can see that it is trading well below what other similar firms are trading for. It also happens to be trading at a meaningful discount to book value. Even if it were to trade at book value, we would be looking at upside of 56.2%. But with management forecasting improvements in cash rent as re-tenanting activities start to bear fruit over the next few quarters, I would say that a premium to book value of some extent is appropriate. Of course, I would not say that the firm should trade anywhere near what these other similar firms are trading for considering the additional leverage. But bridging some of that gap does make sense.

The one big caveat here is that the price to book multiple is a moving target that changes based not only on the overall market value of the company, but based on its shareholder’s equity as well. In the chart below, you can see how the book value per share of the business has deteriorated over the last couple of years. Management has had to write down significant amounts of assets and it has, at times, struggled from a cash flow perspective. The good news is that we are starting to see some stabilization here. Yes, we did see a drop in shareholder’s equity from $4.66 billion in the third quarter to $4.61 billion in the final quarter of the year. But compared to many other previous quarters, especially from the third quarter of 2023 through the final quarter of 2024, we have reached a stage where declines are small.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year before the market opens on April 30th. As we approach that date, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $250.3 million. That would represent a nice increase over the $223.8 million reported a year earlier. But that's not all. As the table below illustrates, both earnings per share and FFO should rise on a year over year basis, taking overall profitability up compared to what it was a year prior. Analysts have not provided estimates when it comes to all of these profitability metrics. But a true test for the company will be the extent to which it can report improvements compared to what it achieved last year. Given the firm's plans and recent operating history, I suspect that it will do quite well.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, it is my sincere belief that, at this point in time, Medical Properties Trust makes for a compelling prospect. I'm not going to stand here and tell you that it is without risk. The firm has had problems in the past, but the good news is that those seem largely resolved. Moving forward, the company seems to be on solid footing, and I believe that upside potential is meaningful. I have reduced my own ownership in the business, but that has more to do with some other opportunities that I have identified than it does the overall quality of the business. At the end of the day, I firmly believe that it still justifies a ‘strong buy’ rating. And I don't see myself downgrading it unless we see upside of at least another 20% or 30% from here.

data.title: Janus Henderson Contrarian Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned -4.88% and the S&P 500® Index returned -4.33%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the consumer discretionary and healthcare sectors detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in the information technology and industrials sectors contributed.

Outlook

Our differentiated investment approach aims to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the broader economy.

Investment environment

  • The equity market started out the year with modest gains, supported by resilient economic growth.
  • Volatility increased in March, after war in the Middle East drove the price of oil over $100 per barrel. Inflation fears pushed long-term bond yields higher while dampening hopes for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts.
  • Artificial intelligence (AI) remained a powerful investment theme, with continued share price gains for companies supplying the AI supply chain. Software stocks declined as investors questioned how AI may reshape market dynamics.

Portfolio review

We remain excited about opportunities around AI, and our conversations with companies across the ecosystem have reinforced our view that demand for AI compute will continue to exceed supply for the foreseeable future. This should fuel ongoing capital investment and opportunities for companies, especially those operating in critical bottlenecks around infrastructure, memory, optical, and power. Such companies include Vertiv Holdings, a top relative contributor for the first quarter. Vertiv is an essential supplier of cooling, power management, and rack systems to AI data centers. It has delivered strong earnings growth, with its orders backlog more than doubling on a year-over-year basis. It has continued to expand its market reach and capabilities through strategic investments, especially in liquid cooling technologies, and it announced a new partnership with NVIDIA (NVDA) to develop advanced solutions to support high-density data centers. Shares rose further on news that Vertiv's stock will be added to the S&P 500 Index, which may draw additional investor attention to the stock.

Lumentum Holdings, another outperformer, is a leading supplier of optical components critical to AI-driven data center networking. The company has several avenues for revenue growth, including sales of optical transceivers, which support the transmission of data through optic cables by transmitting electrical signals into light. It also has a strong foothold in the optical circuit switch market, where Google (GOOGL) is a major customer, and a growing business in co-packaged optics, which are replacing copper as a key connectivity material. Demand for Lumentum's optical components has outpaced supply, resulting in tremendous pricing power and robust earnings growth. Adding to investor excitement, NVIDIA announced that it would invest $2 billion in Lumentum to secure future capacity, further highlighting Lumentum's growing market power as a key provider of mission-critical components to data centers.

Oracle (ORCL) was a relative detractor. While Oracle has continued to gain share in the hyperscale market, with a backlog of more than $500 billion, the stock declined in the first quarter on market concerns over the company's ability to fund its ambitious infrastructure buildout. Concerns about potential disruption in Oracle's legacy software businesses also pressured the stock. We believe these concerns are overstated, as Oracle has multiple avenues for funding growth due to its solid balance sheet and strong operating cash flow. Additionally, we believe its software business is relatively insulated from AI competition given its focus on mission-critical systems. We remain invested in the stock.

Flutter Entertainment, another detractor, is a leading online sports betting and iGaming company. It benefits from global reach, with a strong U.S. presence through its ownership of American gambling company FanDuel. Shares fell in the first quarter after Flutter reported disappointing earnings and guidance, reflecting challenges with promotional generosity and slowing handle growth. These results fueled concerns that traditional sports betting may be losing ground to the growing popularity of the sports prediction markets. Flutter is taking steps to bolster its competitive positioning through its launch of FanDuel Predicts, a predictions platform that will leverage its marketing expertise and economies of scale. We continue to see long-term opportunity for Flutter given its strong market position, global diversification, and commitment to innovation.

Manager outlook

We remain constructive on the broader market outlook, despite near-term uncertainty. While we are mindful of the potential economic effects of higher oil prices, we do not expect events in the Middle East to materially disrupt U.S. economic activity. Consumer spending has held up well, particularly among higher-income households supported by asset appreciation. Tax refunds should provide a modest tailwind for consumers, helping to offset the negative impact of higher gasoline prices. More broadly, capital investment is becoming a stronger engine of economic growth, especially in the AI complex, where hyperscalers are expanding their 2026 capital spending plans.

While we continue to monitor these broader themes along with macroeconomic developments, our primary focus remains on bottom-up stock selection. Our differentiated investment process aims to provide broad exposure across segments, sizes, and styles, while avoiding reliance on any single theme. Through this process, we seek to unlock value through differentiated insights, especially around misunderstood business models, undervalued assets, and unappreciated growth potential. We remain on the lookout for opportunities to take advantage of market volatility, as our long-term investment horizon and independent process allow us to capitalize on opportunities other investors might miss. Through this approach, we hope to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the performance of the broader economy or investment market.

data.title: Intellia Therapeutics And Wall Street Continue To Play Their D&D Game - Data And Doubt
data.description:
I wonder how many people (besides me) have never played Dungeons & Dragons, commonly known as D&D.

That famous role-playing game debuted in 1974. But back then, I was more interested in disco. During that year, Disco broke beyond its underground heritage into the mainstream when “Billboard created its first ‘Disco Action’ chart on October 26, 1974.”

Like many fads, disco eventually died. Many date that from a 7/12/79 Disco Demolition Night event at Chicago’s Comiskey Park. Between games of a baseball doubleheaders, the promoters blew up a crate of vinyl disco records.

D&D, however, kept on going — on and on and on and even into the present. By 2024, about 50 million people are said to have played D&D. And with the game having migrated beyond tabletop toward electronic, it’s still marching on.

I’m not sure how many in the investment community play Dungeons & Dragons. How many have the spare time?

I didn’t mourn the death of disco in 1979. I, too, developed other pressing demands on my time. I started my first stock analysis job at Value Line (VALU) on 12/1/79.

It seems, though, there’s another D&D game that’s become hot — at least between Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) and the analysts-investors who follow or trade in the stock. I call it Data & Doubt.

This new type of D&D game is not (yet) commercially available. But the company and investor teams have been paying for a whie. And the competition really heated up in the past few days

I’ll set the stage by introducing the players and their respective teams…

Team Data — Intellia Therapeutics

I’ve covered NTLA often (most recently on 11/19/25). I’ve done that because I’m fascinated by the company’s methods of developing drugs. It does so using the Crispr Therapeutics (CRSP) gene-editing platform.

This, to me, sounds like the future of medicine.

My wife tells me my ailments are caused by my failure to do what she keeps telling me to do. But scientists suggest genetic factors (gene mutations, gene damage, etc.) are more likely at fault.

It seems logical to assume if we can repair (EDIT) genes, we’d have more substantive permanent cures than we get with medications that merely mask underlying conditions. See generally here and here for more on gene editing.

The latter link is to my first 8/3/24 NTLA writeup. There, I discussed the two approaches to gene editing. Ex vivo means the curative agent is created outside the body from genetic material extracted from the patient at a special treatment center, and then the agent is injected into the patient. In vivo means the curative agent is injected into the patient, in a doctor’s office.

Lately, NTLA has been and still is about two in vivo programs.

Team Doubt — Investors

Normally, one would expect that good data (including news) about a company would lead to better stock performance. But often, this doesn’t happen.

There’s the traditional buy-on-the-rumor-sell-on-the-news phenomenon.

But there’s also just, plain doubt, a tendency to try to poke holes in any sort of positive picture a company paints.

To a degree, we have to respect the latter. It goes directly to the heart of what many would consider good analysis.

But good things taken to excess can turn out bad. Differentiating between good analysis and doubt taken to excess is one of our most challenging tasks.

Recent D&D Events

Round 1: October 2024, Lonvo-z

I described my first exposure to an NTLA-versus-Investors D&D episode on 10/30/24.

I wrote then about the stock having reacted to good clinical-study news about NTLA-2002, now known as Lonvo-z, and its ability to cure HAE.

This is a very serious condition that causes severe and painful swelling. Depending on where it occurs, say, in the patient’s airways, it can lead to death.

This condition is often diagnosed at about age 20. And as of now, it lasts for life — so far…

Lonvo-z aims to correct the genetic defect that causes HAE. If successful, sufferers will be able to replace ongoing often multiple times per week treatments with a one-time doctor’s office infusion.

Back in ’24, NTLA disclosed very successful Phase 2 clinical test results.

Wall Street reacted with a selling blitz —NTLA shares immediately plunged 20.5%. My 10/30/24 article explained why I believed, as I wrote in my headline, that investors’ reasons for bearishness were “flimsy.”

In sum, I believed analysts thought themselves better-suited to evaluate sampling and treatment efficacy than the experts at the FDA. The latter deemed the test results good enough to warrant allowing Lonvo-z to move on the Phase 3 testing. Analysts seemed unconvinced.

Essentially, that whole episode struck me as similar to what football fans encountered in connection with last week’s National Football League (NFL) draft of college players. Looking at sports media and social media, it seemed as if everybody knew better than experienced NFL general managers, scouts and coaches which players teams should draft!

Team Doubt got the first points. But then, the stock recovered and then some. So…

The Round 1 winner - Team Data.

Round 2: July-October 2025, Nex-z

Formerly known as NTLA 2001, this drug aims to treat ATTR. This refers to improperly folded proteins in the liver that can cause all sorts of painful and sometimes dangerous, and possibly fatal, blockages.

Data started out seeming to be fine. But in July 2025, one patient was found to have undesirably high levels of a particular liver enzyme. He was asymptomatic and the condition soon resolved on its own. Phew…

The initial Round 2 winner — Team Data but it was a close come-from-behind victory.

But then, in October 2025, tragedy struck. A study participant experienced troublesome symptoms. His clinical-study manager directed him to go to a hospital. He did. And he passed away shortly thereafter.

Between voluntary action on the part of NTLA and FDA mandate, the Nex-z trial ground to a halt.

There are good reasons to assume Nex-z was not the cause of death. The 80-something patent had other complicating comorbidities.

Further investigation ensued. Additional safety measures (e.g., tighter eligibility screening requirements, additional monitoring) were implemented.

For more details, see my 11/19/25 report.

Subsequently, on 3/2/26, the FDA lifted the clinical hold on testing. NTLA has since gone back to enrolling study participants.

Given the FDA’s go-ahead, it’s tempting to declare victory in favor of Team Data. Supporting that is the reality that drugs on the market that play valuable roles in public health might still be fatal to certain users. (Example: I’d like but can’t get GLP-1 medications since for me, the risk of pancreatic cancer is high.)

But I won’t do that. The facts that surfaced as they surfaced were dire. It would have required an incredible combination of naiveté and callousness to have taken them in stride. So…

The ultimate Round 2 winner - Team Doubt by a landslide

Round 3: Now (April 2026), Lonvo-z

On Friday 4/24/26, NTLA dropped 14%. Then, for a while in the aftermarket, the stock was up around 20%.

At about 8 am on Monday 4/27/26, management delivered another update on Lonvo-z, this one covering Phase 3 test results.

I thought the news was terrific.

Apparently, so too did at least 10 of the 16 participating analysts who, before asking questions, explicitly congratulated NTLA on the excellence of the reported results.

So I assume there would be no way we could repeat the experience of Round 1, when the stock reacted to good data by plunging. This time, the stock would surely hold its aftermarket gains, and move higher when trading opened.

I was wrong.

Actually, most of the post 4/24/26 gain evaporated by the time the market opened on 4/27/26. And the stock closed the day with a 4.3% loss. As of this writing, about an hour into after-market trading, its down another 2%.

So as it appears for now…

In the lead - Team Doubt

Deconstructing Round 3 — The Data

Here’s the number of participants’ HAE attacks per month during the study.

The Lonvo-z and the placebo groups both experienced quick and substantial drops in the number of HAE attacks per month. But as one would expect (hypothesize) at the outset, the drop was much more significant for the Lonvo-z group.

The placebo group, as time passed and the emotional impact of thinking they had Lonvo-z gave way to medical reality, experienced increasing attack frequency.

Look now at what happened after the crossover period (marked by a vertical dashed line). This is when the placebo folks were given the real Lonvo-z. Notice the vigorous and fast decline in their attacks per month, a pattern that essentially mirrored what we saw at the outset for the actual Lonvo-z patients.

I doesn’t take a statistics PhD to recognize that Lonvo-z is making a difference. And the “side effects” experiences of the Lonvo-z and placebo groups were minor and similar to one another.

Here’s an overall summary.

The left portion shows the mean number of monthly attacks. NTLA supplies some significance data on the image. But do you really need it? Anyone should recognize, simply by eyeballing, how excellent that result is.

The right side offers a different perspective. It shows during the meat of the study period (weeks 5 through 28), the percent of participants that were completely attack-free. Again, eyeballing alone should make it clear that this is great.

Deconstructing Round 3 — The Doubts

As with the reaction to Phase 2, analysts again started looking to pick things apart. But this time, the questions didn’t strike me as off base as they did in Round 1. And management’s answers seemed reasonable.

But the stock’s April 24-27 declines tell me the doubts, even if not boldly expressed, remain present. So we’re left to guess and infer as best we can.

It could be a simple buy-the-rumor-sell-the-news situation.

But my sense is that analysts are still looking for more perfection than may be feasible.

For example, one analyst wondered why the 62% of patients being attack free in weeks 5-28 wasn’t higher, and how things would look in the real world when patients know they aren’t in a clinical test.

The answer was…

I think you're referring to the attack-free rate of 62%. So it's a challenging endpoint, meaning that you basically have to be perfect that no one reports any HAE symptoms at all. And that can be -- it's a high bar in part because HAE symptoms are so variable. And most -- really all studies have relied on patient-reported outcomes. So by that, I mean, we know HAE, for instance, causes abdominal attacks. And so if a patient reports abdominal pain suggestive of an HAE attack, the investigator must have to adjudicate that. But we tend to trust our patients. And if they report that as an HAE symptom, we review it, but it may be reported as such, even if there are other possible explanations for that abdominal pain.

So that's all to say that the symptoms are highly variable. And so any symptom that is suggestive of HAE or receives HAE treatment during the course of the study period will likely be recorded as an HAE attack. So I personally think that's the reason that we don't see 100% attack-free in really any of the trials that have been done even with ver effective medicines…. The second part of your question, how will it perform in the real world? I can't predict the future. But what I can tell you is that nearly all of the HAE preventative therapies that we prescribe now have generally outperformed in the real world compared to the clinical trial.

That’s reasonably representative of most of the clinically-oriented questions asked on the update call.

Beyond that, as I listened to the call live earlier in the day, I sensed particular concern about the desirability of having a gene-edited treatment at all. How many patients were really unhappy with existing treatment protocols?

I’m not sure the number of such questions was exorbitant. My sense may have been influenced by the vehemence with which they were expressed, and by my own belief that this line of questioning was, indeed, ridiculous.

I wonder about the ages and health of the analysts on the call. Have any of them had medical issues that required lifetime multiple-times-per-week treatments?

We don’t have to quiz HAE patients as to how they feel about hypothetical (to them) alternatives. Common sense alone should tell us a one-time doctor’s office infusion will be much preferred, even if sufferers today who don’t yet have the ability to choose it don’t yet realize what their answers will be in the future, when things get real.

Round 3 Outcome

As with Round 1, I’m casting my vote for Team Data. I think the Phase 3 results were stupendous. And I see the doubts as having ranged from neutral to absurd.

NTLA sees it this way. That’s why it’s preparing for a commercial launch of Lonvo-z in the first half of 2027.

This will require finalization of a rolling BLA. This is Biologics License Application that is submitted in phases, as various regulatory milestones are completed. Right now, NTLA is on track for finalization early next year.

But my opinion won’t influence the stock. And so far, neither has clinical data nor the FDA’s opinion. Mr. Market is the final arbiter. And so far…

In the lead - Team Doubt

Go figure.

Risk

Obviously, with any clinical stage biotech, there’s a ton of clinical execution risk. This isn’t exclusive to NTLA. It’s part and parcel of the business.

So, too, is financial risk. We know for sure companies like this can’t stay in business unless they keep getting outside capital.

That’s exactly what NTLA has been doing.

Ignore the revenues. Those are non-commercial research-like payments. Focus on the cash. It came down from its peak. That’s no surprise. Companies like NTLA burn cash and will continue doing so until commercial operations get going.

They could fund themselves by borrowing. I’m glad, however, NTLA hasn’t gone that route. The last thing in the world a company like this needs is fixed interest-expense burdens.

So NTLA has kept going back to the equity markets to sell more stock. That’s the right approach for it. As long as suppliers of such capital remain confident in a company’s future, they’ll keep the money spigot flowing.

And by the way, when it comes to raising equity, please, please, please do yourself a favor and ignore a lot of what you see from time to time from those who complain about dilution. This is not a simplistic computation. You can’t just add more shares to the calculation. You also have to consider how much extra profit the company will eventually earn from the new capital. (That’s the hard part many commentators ignore.)

Ultimately, NTLA management believes it now has enough cash to get to mid 2027. And I believe it will get more when it needs it, especially since that would be the time when Lonvo-z will be starting to go commercial. If Next-z is back on it’s feet by then, so much the better.

What to do About NTLA Stock

NTLA does not yet have the sort of commerce-based fundamentals that can support use of conventional valuation metrics.

So a decision today on the stock depends on how Mr. Market, as judge and referee, is assessing the contest between Team Data and Team Doubt.

The price chart gives us some insight here.

Looking at the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) and the 50-day EMA, we see that Mr. Market has lately had a soft spot in favor of Team Data (as expressed by the 10-day EMA being higher). But it’s been only mild and inconsistent.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) are more definitive. Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both indicators had some bullish moments in the recent past. For now, they’re solidly ensconced in the bearish Team Doubt camp. Sellers are more motivated than buyers.

But I’m not inclined to call it a day and stop here. We’ve seen how things in this news-driven stock can turn on a dime. Team Doubt has been strong lately. But I can see Team Data putting up a much tougher fight in the months ahead (due to more progress toward commercializing Lonvo-z, and Nex-z putting more distance between it and its recent woes).

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

Right now, I see stalemate for NTLA in Team Data versus Team Doubt. That suggests neutral market performance.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by continuing to rate NTLA as a “Hold.”

data.title: Best Dividend Aristocrats: April 2026
data.description:
2026 Review

The Dividend Aristocrats had a terrible March collectively, and are thus far not enjoying as robust a rebound as the broad equity market. The early outperformance the Aristocrats have built on the S&P 500 has all but faded here in April. What's also interesting is the range of returns for individual Aristocrats. In March the margin between the best Aristocrat, Exxon Mobil (XOM), and the worst, McCormick (MKC), was in excess of 40%. In April thus far this gap is equivalently high, except now Exxon is the worst performing Aristocrat, with Nucor Corporation (NUE) leading the charge.

The ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrat ETF (NOBL) was down 7.04% in March and is up 2.04% through April 27th. While the S&P 500, measured by the SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY), was 4.93% down in March, and is up 9.91% in April thus far.
