data.title: Pagaya: Capitalizing On Private Credit's Great Rotation While Operating Leverage Shines
data.description:
Since reporting Q4 earnings, shares of Pagaya Technologies Ltd. (PGY) is down 27% after missing top-line estimates for the first time in 7 quarters and posting conservative guidance for FY 2026. Pagaya's earnings results and conservative guidance were driven by the company tightening its box and becoming more selective in its approvals due to the uncertainties brought by the ongoing geopolitical instability and inflation fears.

Despite this uncertain environment, Pagaya's operating leverage continues to shine as the company reported sequential improvements to its net margin and free cash flow in Q4 despite shedding $100-150 million in network volume. In my opinion, the scale of the company's operating leverage could drive earnings upside this year, especially as its exposure to non-performing loan vintages from 2023 and prior continues to decline as these vintages approach maturity.

Meanwhile, Pagaya's robust underwriting, evidenced by its high repeat borrower rate, is a key differentiator amidst the ongoing state of the private credit market. With private credit rotating into consumer loans to reduce their exposure to direct corporate lending's jitters, the company could see strong momentum for its ABS transactions and potentially secure additional forward flow agreements. As is, the company appears to be benefiting from this market dynamic, with its ABS issuances this year seeing a wave of new investors.

Although Pagaya's fundamentals remain robust, expanding its partner network and guiding for strong bottom-line growth in FY 2026, it still trades at a discount relative to its peers, suggesting significant reversal potential in the near term. In light of these factors, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating for Pagaya with a base case $23 price target, implying 68% upside potential from current levels.

Operating Leverage Offsets Credit Tightening

In late Q4 2025, Pagaya decided to tighten its box and reduce its exposure to higher-risk segments such as single-family rentals, which had zero volume in the quarter, and borrower pools. This decision was not due to deteriorating credit performance but due to the company's lending partners' lower risk appetite amidst the ongoing geopolitical uncertainties and inflation fears.

In fact, the credit performance of Pagaya-enabled loans was better than management's forecast in FY 2025 as credit-related impairments for the full year came at $95.4 million. This figure was lower than management's forecast between $100-150 million and 76.9% lower than the $412.1 million incurred in the prior year. While the credit-related impairments still represented 11.3% of Pagaya's average loan balance in FY 2025, $845.5 million, it should be noted that the company is still exposed to the non-performing vintages of 2023 and prior.

At the end of FY 2025, the company held $181 million in 2023 and prior loan vintages, representing 19.2% of the loans held on its balance sheet. As these loans reach their maturities, Pagaya could be set to report significantly lower credit-related impairments. With that in mind, cumulative net losses (CNL) for personal loan vintages from the second half of 2024 through the first half of 2025 are trending 30-40% lower than peak levels in 2021, while auto loan CNLs over the same period are trending 50-70% lower than 2022 vintages, per management in the Q4 earnings call.

Still, Pagaya's decision to tighten its model had a material impact on its Q4 earnings results as it shed $100-150 million in network volume in December and anticipates the decision to represent a $1.5 billion headwind to its network volume in FY 2026. This decision saw the company miss top-line estimates for the first time in 7 quarters while also marking its first sequential network volume decline during its seasonally strongest quarter since 2022.

During the quarter, Pagaya's network volume declined 4.2% sequentially from $2.8 billion to $2.68 billion, driving a 5.5% decline in fee revenue from $339.9 million to $321 million as its take rate slightly contracted from 12.1% to 12%. In addition, Pagaya's fee revenue less production costs (FRLPC) declined 6% over the same period, from $139.3 million to $131 million, outpacing its fee revenue decline, as production costs decreased at a slower pace compared to fee revenue at 5.2%, falling from $200.6 million to $190 million.

The main contributor to the slower decline in production costs is that 50% of the company's fee revenue came from products other than its core Decline Monetization product. These products typically carry a lower FRLPC margin profile as they are still new products and haven't reached Decline Monetization's maturity yet. As a result, Pagaya's FRLPC as a percentage of network volume came in at 4.9% in Q4, down from 5%, the higher end of management's target, in the prior quarter.

Despite the top-line pressure brought by Pagaya's tightening, its operating leverage shone bright in Q4. During the quarter, the company's core operating costs, which include technology, data, product development, sales and marketing, and G&A, declined 6.6% sequentially from $69.6 million to $65 million, outpacing the top-line decline, and represented 49.7% of its FRLPC compared to 50% in Q3.

Pagaya's operating leverage saw it report a relatively flat operating income sequentially, despite the top-line decline, at $79.7 million compared to $80 million, representing a 23.8% operating margin compared to 22.8% in the prior quarter, a 100 basis point expansion. In turn, the company was able to report a 52.1% sequential increase to its GAAP income from $22.5 million to $34.3 million. This represents a 10.2% net margin, a significant expansion from 6.4% in the prior quarter.

These efficiencies reflected on Pagaya's operating cash flow, which increased 19% sequentially from $67 million to $79.8 million as it converted nearly 61% of its FRLPC into operating cash flow compared to 48% in the prior quarter. In this way, the company generated $76.7 million in free cash flow in Q4 at a 22.9% margin, 20% higher than Q3's $63.8 million at an 18.2% margin.

The scale of Pagaya's operating leverage is set to be the primary driver for its bottom-line growth in FY 2026. In its full-year guidance, the company anticipates full-year network volume to grow 15.1% YoY at the midpoint to $12.13 billion, outpacing revenue, which management anticipates to grow 14.3% at the midpoint to $1.49 billion. This suggests that the company is positioned to see a decline in its take rate, which would see its FRLPC as a percentage of network volume trend near the midpoint of its 4-5% target.

Despite this, Pagaya is guiding for adjusted EBITDA to grow 17.3% YoY at the midpoint of its guidance, $435 million, implying an adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to 29.2% compared to 28.5% in FY 2025. At the same time, the company is guiding for GAAP income to grow 53.6% YoY at the midpoint, $125 million, which also implies a net margin expansion to 8.4% compared to 6.3% in FY 2025, highlighting the scale of its operating leverage amidst the anticipated top-line pressure.

Expanding Network to Drive Earnings Upside

With Pagaya guiding for network volume growth in FY 2026 despite shedding $1.5 billion in volume due to tightening its model, it should be noted that the primary growth driver for the company is its expanding network. From Q4 2025 up until its Q4 earnings call in late February, Pagaya onboarded three new partners to its network.

These partners include Achieve for its personal loan network, Global Lending Services for its auto loan network, and an unnamed major buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) provider for its POS network. Since then, the company has onboarded another partner to its network in early March, with another four or five partners in the pipeline to be onboarded this year.

With Pagaya exiting 2025 with 30 lending partners in its network, these new partners could push its network to nearly 40 lending partners by the end of the year. Accordingly, these new partners will be crucial for the company achieving its network volume guidance following its decision to tighten its model.

On that note, the impact of these new partners on Pagaya's network volume is likely to be minimal this year due to the typical ramp-up period required to fully integrate and scale loan volumes from new partner originations. This is a promising fact in light of management's guidance for 15% YoY network volume growth this year, as it suggests that growth could further accelerate in FY 2027 as these new partnerships mature.

Another positive aspect of Pagaya's partner expansion is its ability to offer its entire product suite to new partners immediately. As mentioned earlier, around 50% of the company's network volume in Q4 came from products other than its core Decline Monetization. These products include Affiliate Optimizer Engine, Direct Marketing Engine, and Dual Look, all of which are designed to help partners gain new customers and increase their loan conversion rates.

In my opinion, the initial lower network volumes from new partners and the lower margin profile of Pagaya's newer products reinforce my view of its FRLPC as a percentage of network volume trending near the midpoint of its 4-5% target in FY 2026. That said, I expect the company's FRLPC as a percentage of network volume could approach the higher end of its target range by late FY 2027 as its new partnerships mature and more partners adopt its newer products.

Despite the expected FRLPC margin pressure in FY 2026, the evident scale of Pagaya's operating leverage means that a majority of the incremental FRLPC growth in FY 2026 is likely to flow directly to its bottom line, supporting management's guidance for 53.6% YoY GAAP income growth. As is, the company's non-GAAP core operating expenses have been constant over the past 2 quarters at $47 million, highlighting its efficient cost management.

On another note, Pagaya has the potential to report lower than forecasted credit-related impairments in FY 2026, potentially driving further earnings upside, even with the anticipated higher volume from its new partners and the higher adoption of its product suite. With the company becoming more selective in the loans it approves, the incremental network volume from its new partners is likely to be for lower-risk borrower pools.

This suggests that its 2026 loan vintages are likely to outperform its loan vintages from the second half of 2024 through the first half of 2025, which are already outperforming its 2023 and prior vintages. With that in mind, Pagaya is likely to see a major reduction to its 2023 and prior loan vintages this year, considering that its personal loans typically carry terms of 36 and 60 months, meaning that a significant portion of these vintages will mature this year. In my opinion, the balance of Pagaya's 2023 and prior loan vintages could decline by 30% this year, thus reducing its exposure to these high-risk vintages.

Pagaya's expected lower exposure to 2023 and prior loan vintages combined with a conservative approach to approving loans this year suggests that it could report lower credit-related impairments this year compared to last year, which already came below management's forecast. Since the company's FY 2026 guidance is based on $100-150 million in credit-related impairments, reporting lower credit-related impairments than last year could push Pagaya's GAAP income near the high end of its guidance, $150 million, or even past its guidance.

Tightening Private Credit is an Opportunity

On another note, Pagaya has the potential to benefit from the ongoing weakness in the private credit market. The tightening private credit market is driven by its high exposure to the tech sector, driving fears among investors regarding valuations and transparency following the high-profile bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor late last year.

Additionally, fears of AI disrupting traditional software business models raised investors' fears of these companies repaying their private credit loans. As is, the use of paid-in-kind (PIK) interest is increasing, and $620 billion in debt issued in the low-rate environment of 2020 and 2021 is expected to be refinanced at a materially higher cost between 2026 and 2028.

These growing concerns have led to a spike in redemption requests from investors across several flagship funds, including Blue Owl (OWL), a key Pagaya funding partner with an active $2.4 billion forward flow agreement that expires in early 2027. Although private credit's direct lending arms are suffering, asset-backed financing (ABF) is emerging as an area of growth to offset direct lending's weakness.

According to Moody's 2026 Private Credit Outlook, private credit investments are shifting from corporate lending to ABF, especially in consumer loans and data infrastructure credit. This shift is expected to lead the private credit market's growth this year as ABF loans offer lower credit risk compared to direct corporate lending.

ABF loans' lower credit risk profile stems from the risk being spread across a vast loan pool as well as being backed by tangible assets with predictable cash flows. On the other hand, direct corporate lending's credit risk is centered on borrowers' EV and cash flow, and these loans are backed by corporate assets whose values depend on businesses' ongoing operations.

Against this backdrop, it appears that Pagaya is benefiting from this shift thanks to the volume of its ABS transactions so far this year. Year-to-date, Pagaya has raised $3.5 billion spanning 7 ABS transactions under its personal loan PAID program and auto loan RPM program. This suggests that Pagaya is on track to report another record year in terms of ABS funding, as its raised amount YTD represents 41% of the $8.5 billion it raised in FY 2025.

More crucially, all of Pagaya's ABS transactions this year included the participation of new investors. In fact, the company's PAID 2026-1 transaction, which was oversubscribed even after being upsized from $600 million to $800 million, attracted 4 new investors, the same number of investors the company added to its ABS network in the entirety of Q4 2025.

In my opinion, Pagaya's ability to attract new investors amidst private credit's ongoing jitters could be tied to its high repeat borrower rate. In its Q4 presentation, the company shared that 25-30% of its enabled loans are to borrowers who had a prior Pagaya-enabled loan in the last 3 years. This means that a significant portion of the company's network volume in 2025 is anchored by borrowers who were stress-tested during the 2022-2023 period that is still representing a drag on the company's financials.

This significantly de-risks Pagaya's loan vintages from the second half of 2024 and thereafter, in my opinion, since it already has the repayment history of these borrowers. Given that a repeat borrower's probability of default is statistically lower than a first-time borrower with the same FICO score, Pagaya can approve loans to these borrowers with higher confidence and lower credit enhancement requirements for its ABS transactions. As such, Pagaya's high repeat borrower rate reduces its credit risk while lowering its cost of capital, positioning it to report lower credit-related impairments on its second half of 2024 and thereafter loan vintages.

Additionally, Pagaya's ABS transactions this year included two resecuritization transactions under its PAID program and the inaugural resecuritization transaction under its RPM program, accounting for $1.2 billion of its total ABS issuances. These resecuritizations have a favorable risk profile for investors, as the loans included in these transactions typically have a weighted average seasoning of around 24 months, meaning that these loans have an established performance history.

Since these resecuritization transactions lower Pagaya's risk retention requirements on the resecuritized loans, they effectively reduce its cost of capital, leading to an improved FRLPC. Furthermore, these transactions unlock capital invested in lower-quality loans originated in the first half of 2024 and prior, thus allowing Pagaya to reallocate these funds to new, higher-quality network volume this year, further reducing its credit risk.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, Pagaya appears to be trading at a massive discount relative to other fintechs despite its robust fundamentals. At its share price of $13.59, Pagaya is trading at a non-GAAP P/E of 4.75 relative to analysts FY 2026 EPS target of $2.86, though these estimates could be extremely conservative given the company's expected network volume growth and strong operating leverage.

In comparison, companies like Upstart (UPST), SoFi (SOFI), LendingClub (LC), Affirm (AFRM), and Sezzle (SEZL) are trading at the following forward non-GAAP P/E ratios.

Furthermore, Pagaya is trading significantly below its sector median non-GAAP P/E of 23.78. This suggests that Pagaya is trading at an 80% discount to its sector median and a 74.5% discount to the average P/E of the aforementioned peer group.

With that in mind, the following YCharts shows that Pagaya's current non-GAAP forward P/E is near the bottom it reached in the first half of 2025, despite its improved fundamentals over that period.

In my opinion, Pagaya's current discount suggests a potential re-rating in the near term to reach a non-GAAP P/E between 8 and 10. In that scenario, my target multiple is 8, which implies a price target of $23, representing 68% upside from current levels.

Such a re-rating could be driven by Pagaya's upcoming Q1 earnings results on May 7th if it exceeds its network volume guidance between $2.5-2.7 billion and its GAAP income guidance between $15-35 million.

Meanwhile, if Pagaya beats its guidance, raises its full-year guidance, and loosens its model, indicating an improved macro outlook, the stock could re-rate to a forward non-GAAP P/E between 12 and 14, a level it has reached multiple times over the past 2 years. In that scenario, my target multiple is 12, which implies a price target of $34, representing 153% upside potential from current levels.

However, if Pagaya's Q1 results are within guidance while maintaining its full-year guidance or if new partners' ramps are slower than expected, the stock could drop post earnings and test the $10 support level. This scenario implies a potential 26% downside from the stock's share price.

Technical Analysis

My view of a Pagaya re-rating in the near term is further supported by its stock's price action. The TrendSpider chart above shows that Pagaya is in the midst of a textbook fishhook reversal pattern on the weekly. This structure can be seen with the stock consolidating near the $10.40 52-week low it reached post the Q4 earnings selloff, followed by a steady recovery towards the key $15 resistance level, accompanied with the RSI curving upward after dipping into oversold territory.

While the stock failed to break out of the $15 resistance twice over the last 2 trading weeks, the probability of a strong weekly close above that level is high, in my opinion, with the MACD line on the verge of a bullish crossover above the signal line, as the histogram reflects weakening selling momentum. Such a crossover could trigger a breakout of the $15 resistance and suggest a move to test the $20 resistance, which coincides with the company's upcoming Q1 earnings release.

Risks

Although I remain bullish on Pagaya's fundamentals and its near-term reversal potential, there are several risks worth noting that could undermine my thesis. From a macro level, March's CPI spike to 3.3% compared to 2.4% in February, driven by a 10.9% MoM surge in energy costs, is a key risk to watch as it could impact Pagaya's cost of capital and network volume. If higher energy costs persist, causing inflation to remain elevated above the Fed's 2% target, current expectations of a rate cut later this year may not materialize, while also introducing the risk of potential rate hikes to curb high inflation.

In that case, a wave of layoffs could push unemployment rates above 4.5%, which would increase the probability of default across Pagaya's loan pools and thus prolong Pagaya's credit tightening timeline. Moreover, Pagaya could be forced to increase the note coupon on its ABS transactions to drive institutional demand, which would reduce its take rate and effectively compress its FRLPC margin, which would eliminate any gains brought by its operating leverage scale.

Another macro risk worth noting is persistent weakness in the private credit market. While Pagaya appears to be benefiting from the current environment, if redemption requests remain elevated at Blue Owl specifically, the private credit firm could be forced to reduce loan purchases under the active $2.4 billion forward flow agreement to free up liquidity. Such an environment may also drive Blue Owl to not renew the current forward flow agreement with Pagaya when it expires in early 2027. In both cases, Pagaya's risk exposure would effectively increase if it is unable to secure similar agreements with other institutions.

As for Pagaya itself, a key risk to watch over the coming quarters is FRLPC margin dilution following its decision to tighten its model. If a majority of the company's network volume comes from its newer products, its FRLPC margin could trend below the midpoint of its target. Similarly, a slower-than-expected ramp-up of Pagaya's new partners may also dilute its FRLPC margin below the midpoint of its target, especially after shedding $1.5 billion in expected network volume this year. In both cases, any incremental network volume gains may not reflect on Pagaya's FRLPC in dollar terms, thus limiting any potential earnings upside from the company's operating leverage scale.

Conclusion

In summary, Pagaya presents an attractive risk-to-reward profile with its current valuation reminiscent of last year's despite its improved fundamentals over that period. While the company tightened its model in Q4, the scale of its operating leverage shone bright, with accelerating GAAP income and free cash flow generation sequentially despite top-line pressures.

Pagaya's operating leverage scale is set to be the primary driver for its anticipated bottom-line growth, as its top-line is expected to witness similar pressure this year due to its expanding partner network and the increasing adoption of its newer products. While these factors could compress Pagaya's FRLPC margin as a percentage of network volume, incremental FRLPC gains in dollar terms could mostly flow through to its bottom line thanks to its operating leverage scale.

On another note, Pagaya is emerging as a beneficiary from ongoing private credit woes in direct corporate lending, with private credit funds shifting to ABF structures due to their lower risk profile. This structural shift can be seen in the volume of the company's ABS transactions this year, representing 41% of its ABS funding in FY 2025, all of which saw the participation of new investors.

Furthermore, Pagaya's strong resecuritization momentum this year, raising $1.2 billion YTD, could be a crucial factor in reducing its credit-related impairments this year. With the company reducing its exposure to loans originated in 2023 and prior through resecuritizations and tightening its model, I expect it to report credit-related impairments lower than last year's $95.4 million.

Considering that the company's FY 2026 guidance is based on a credit-related impairment forecast between $100 million and $150 million, such an outperformance could see Pagaya exceed its full-year GAAP income guidance of between $100 million and $150 million. In light of these factors, I'm reiterating my strong buy rating for Pagaya with a base case price target of $23, implying 68% upside potential from its current valuation.

data.title: Heartland Small Cap Value Plus Strategy Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • After market breadth began to improve late last year, the economy has been showing signs of broadening out.
  • However, the outbreak of war in Iran and rising oil prices raises questions of whether this trend may be hitting a pause.
  • In uncertain times like this, we are looking for signs that give us confidence in our security selection, such as stock buybacks, insider buying, and dividend increases.

First Quarter Market Discussion

Improving market breadth, which started to take shape at the end of last year, carried into the first quarter, with the Russell 2000® Index up 0.89% versus the S&P 500 Index which fell 4.33%.

The outbreak of war in Iran, however, seems to have clouded the picture. Since the military action began at the end of February, the broad market and small-cap companies have begun to slump. Yet history tells us not to overreact to short-term and unpredictable geopolitical developments. Time is better spent focusing on those factors that actually drive the markets in the long run. On that basis, there remains hope.

For instance, the underlying economy now appears to be broadening as well, confirming what improved market breadth had been signaling. Heading into the year, there were concerns about the uneven nature of the recovery, with high-net-worth households thriving thanks in part to growing asset values, while working class consumers have been struggling due to persistent inflationary pressures and affordability concerns. This divide has been referred to as the ‘K-shaped’ recovery.

Anecdotally, however, some companies that serve households in the bottom part of the K have reported seeing signs of green shoots forming this year. At the very least, some of the stresses on this segment of the economy no longer appear to be worsening. Lower-income households should also see a lift with the impending arrival of tax rebate checks, which disproportionately benefit the working class, though rising prices at the pump since the start of the war may have a counteracting effect.

Another sign of the broadening economy can be found in the health of the Industrials sector. For more than two years, the Institute for Supply Management’s Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) has been indicating contraction in factory activity. So far this year, though, the PMI for manufacturing has enjoyed back-to-back readings above 50, marking the first signs of growth in back-to-back months in four years. Even better, 90% of the individual PMI readings across various parts of the economy signaled strength during the first quarter. These are levels we have not seen since early 2022 (see the chart below).

Source: FactSet Research Systems Inc. (FDS) via Piper Sandler Monthly data 12/31/1975 to 3/31/2026. The data in this chart shows a diffusion index highlighting how many of the US PMIs (Dallas, Richmond, Philly, Chicago, Kansas, Empire) are currently in expansion relative to the total possible number. Regional US Purchasing Managers' Indices (PMIs) are monthly, localized economic indicators surveying manufacturing or service sector purchasing managers about business conditions. The ISM Services Index (formerly Non-Manufacturing Index or NMI) is a monthly economic indicator based on surveys of purchasing managers across U.S. The ISM Manufacturing Index, or Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI), is a monthly economic indicator based on surveys of purchasing managers at roughly 300–400 U.S. manufacturing firms. The S&P Global US Services PMI (formerly Markit) is a key monthly economic indicator gauging the private service sector's health, with a January 2026 reading of 52.70, indicating expansion. The S&P Global (formerly Markit) US Manufacturing PMI indicates the health of the manufacturing sector, with a January 2026 reading of 52.4, signaling expansion. All indices are unmanaged. It is not possible to invest in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

However, geopolitical risks could pose a threat to the trends that have been working in favor of small-cap investors. In times of uncertainty, it’s important to be confident in the companies we choose. Capital allocation decisions are insightful here. A good indicator of management’s conviction in their operational performance, strategic plan, and financial decision-making can come in the form of share buybacks, dividend increases and insider buying. Nearly 90% of the companies in our portfolio have active buybacks while 60% are currently boosting their dividend payouts and more than 1/3rd have seen insider buying. In all, 69% of our holdings are flashing at least two of these three important signs.

Another source of confidence comes from our adherence to our 10 Principles of Value Investing™. These guidelines require us to focus on fundamentals factors such valuations, balance sheet strength, earnings dynamics, and sound business strategies, among other considerations. Low prices, low debt, and high earnings growth potential help build in a margin of safety that is attractive to value investors like us but is particularly useful when uncertainty and volatility are on the rise.

Attribution Analysis & Portfolio Activity

The Value Plus Strategy gained 4.94% in the first quarter, compared with the 4.96% gain for the Russell 2000® Value Index. Stock selection accounted for some of our performance in the quarter, as our selection effect was positive in several sectors, led by Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary, and Health Care. By contrast, our selection effect was negative in Financials, Energy, and Real Estate.

The first quarter was a challenging period for artificial intelligence stocks, many of which stumbled as risk aversion returned to the market amid fears of a possible AI bubble forming. But for small-cap tech stocks, the first quarter proved to be quite strong in part because this group had already suffered setbacks last year and, for the most part, valuations never got out of hand.

An example is Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (SIMO). A year ago, shares of the leading maker of memory components used in PCs, smartphones, data centers, and industrial and auto applications sold off amid a variety of concerns. They included tariffs, consumer spending worries, and questions over whether investors might be overestimating the capex needs of large-scale cloud service providers known as hyperscalers. At the time, we remained committed to the stock because we believed the company was in the early days of a re-rating process, as SIMO had been making a push away from trailing-edge, lower-margin consumer electronics into higher-margin, leading-edge applications driven by hyperscaler demands.

What a difference a year makes. In the first quarter, the stock was a contributor to our outperformance, as consumer spending has held up and hyperscalers continue to indicate robust datacenter capex growth. In their fourth-quarter conference call, management reiterated the firm’s outlook for their PC and smartphone end markets and the growth prospects for their data center storage components, which are expected to drive margins substantially higher.

Yet in our opinion, SIMO remains meaningfully undervalued versus our current price target. The stock currently trades at $117, but we believe the company should be valued at $185. That’s based on a multiple of 15X EBITDA plus an anticipated $160 million cash settlement from SIMO’s ongoing arbitration with MaxLinear (MXL) surrounding the termination of a proposed merger agreement more than two years ago.

In this K-shaped recovery, shares of Wyndham Hotels (WH) have been punished as revenues for hotels that operate in the midscale and economy space have lagged behind upscale peers such as Marriott (MAR) or Hilton (HLT). This isn’t so surprising, as inflation has eaten into the consumer budgets while the weak manufacturing economy also impacted Wyndham’s grey and blue collar business travel base.Yet a broadening economy and improving PMI should benefit Wyndham’s base of business travelers and middle income customers. The global franchisor of hotels such as Wyndham, Days Inn, Laquinta, Ramada, and Super 8 should also see an incremental boost in demand in some of its key markets thanks to key events this year, including the World Cup in North America, the 250th birthday celebration for the U.S., and the 100th anniversary of Route 66.

Wyndham shares are valued at just 11-12x EBITDA, which compares favorably to Marriott and Hilton, which trade at 17-20x EBITDA. Even better, management has been actively buying back the company’s shares while consistently boosting its dividend payout, hitting two of our three capital allocation priorities.

One of the biggest detractors to our strategy’s performance in the quarter was Stifel Financial Corp. (SF), which provides brokerage, investment banking, investment advisory, and related financial services.

Shares of the global wealth manager began selling off in early February as the market expressed concerns around AI’s ability to disrupt their business. Then, the spike in interest rates, driven by concerns about higher inflation and oil prices, further weighed on the stock as the market sold off in March, impacting total client assets and investment banking prospects during a period of potentially “higher for longer” rates.

We believe Stifel should still be able to grow revenues and expand margins for the foreseeable future as the company continues to execute on organic asset growth. The company ranked no. 1 in JD Power’s Advisor Satisfaction study for the third consecutive year. As a result, Stifel continues to attract new financial advisors to its platform. Meanwhile, AI should be a tailwind for operational efficiency as it allows Stifel to automate routine tasks like compliance checks, client onboarding, and producing reports. This should help boost advisor productivity and better client retention.

Yet with price/earnings ratio of 11 times 2026 consensus EPS estimates, we believe Stifel is trading at an attractive valuation, especially for a business enjoying durable low-double-digit EPS growth.

Outlook

While recent geopolitical developments have introduced new uncertainties to the markets, investing is never certain. Our job as active managers is to focus on our security selection process. Our 10 Principles of Value Investing™ help guide us to underappreciated and overlooked stocks, many of which have already taken their lumps and trade at attractive levels. They also direct us to well-managed businesses with sound balance sheets and strategies, which build in an added margin of safety that will be particularly useful if volatility persists. If uncertainty wanes, though, we believe our bottom-up stock selection process should position us to take advantage of the broadening economy and improving market breadth.

Thank you for your continued trust and confidence in us.
